title: Iron Dust
desc: Fine metal dust is building up on the lower catwalks again, which makes every gauge harder to read and every bootstep less wise.

steps.text:
Iron Pillar sheds itself in ways both dramatic and tedious. The dramatic part is easy to notice: sparks, scrap, tremors, bent plates. The tedious part is the dust. Fine metallic powder gathers on catwalk seams, clings to gauge housings, and creeps toward every corner where the field can persuade it to collect. Left alone, it turns good footing slippery and careful instruments unreadable, which is exactly the sort of slow failure the UnderWorld specializes in creating by neglect.

The dust hand lifts a resin broom and points to the lower loop. "Sweep the catwalk seams into the trap pans if you want the broad circuit," she says. "Or clean the gauge faces and magnet markers if you prefer the fiddlier work. Either way, I would like the shift to stop walking on powdered mistakes."

options.label: Sweep the catwalk seams into the trap pans
Clean the gauge faces and magnet markers

steps.text:
The dust moves oddly under the broom, skidding toward the pillar in little jerks whenever the field catches it, then surrendering all at once into the resin pan when the angle finally favors you. You work the lower loop a section at a time, pulling the gray glitter from seam teeth, heel grooves, and chain plates until the walkway stops sparkling with danger and goes back to looking merely industrial.

The dust hand drags a boot across one cleaned span and seems satisfied by the amount of friction returned to the world. "Good. I prefer my hazards large enough to deserve proper swearing."

options.label: Empty the final pan and leave the lower loop clean

steps.text:
The gauge faces sit behind little glass windows and side by side with painted magnet markers that indicate how far a tool or bracket ought to be trusted in any given section. Dust has filmed the glass and dulled the painted bands until half the instruments look equally unhelpful. You wipe the windows clear, brush the filings from the marker seams, and repaint one red band that had nearly vanished under the gray bloom.

Once cleaned, the gauges start telling on the pillar again. Needles show actual drift instead of blur. Warning bands recover their contrast. The dust hand studies the restored row and gives a satisfied grunt. "Better. If the pillar wants to kill anyone today, let it do so honestly."

options.label: Seal the paint pot and leave the gauges readable
